title: Pensions and Fertility: Microeconomic Evidence
mechanism: Abstract confirms a DID/IV/event-study design exploiting Brazil's quasi-experimental pension expansion to rural workers, finding reform reduces childbearing propensity by 8% — textbook Tier 1 causal identification with OAS-fertility as the sole primary question.
scoreRationale: Abstract confirms a DID/IV/event-study design exploiting Brazil's quasi-experimental pension expansion to rural workers, finding reform reduces childbearing propensity by 8% — textbook Tier 1 causal identification with OAS-fertility as the sole primary question.

title: The Old-Age Security Motive for Fertility: Evidence from the Extension of Social Pensions in Namibia
mechanism: Abstract confirms quasi-experimental DID exploiting extension of social pensions in Namibia across regions and cohorts to estimate causal effect of pensions on fertility; gold-standard identification, OAS-fertility is the sole research question.
scoreRationale: Abstract confirms quasi-experimental DID exploiting extension of social pensions in Namibia across regions and cohorts to estimate causal effect of pensions on fertility; gold-standard identification, OAS-fertility is the sole research question.

title: What Explains Fertility? Evidence from Italian Pension Reforms
mechanism: Natural experiment using Italian pension reforms to identify causal effect on fertility — the primary research question; highest possible score on all dimensions.
scoreRationale: Natural experiment using Italian pension reforms to identify causal effect on fertility — the primary research question; highest possible score on all dimensions.

title: The impact of long-term care insurance on family fertility behaviour: evidence from China
mechanism: Quasi-experimental DiD exploiting China's long-term care insurance pilot rollout to estimate causal effect on fertility behavior, with OAS-fertility link as the primary research question — top-scoring paper in the batch.
scoreRationale: Quasi-experimental DiD exploiting China's long-term care insurance pilot rollout to estimate causal effect on fertility behavior, with OAS-fertility link as the primary research question — top-scoring paper in the batch.

title: The Introduction of Bismarck's Social Security System and its Effects on Marriage and Fertility in Prussia
mechanism: Natural experiment exploiting Bismarck's 1881 social insurance introduction to test causal effect on Prussian marriage and fertility 1881–1910 — directly targets the OAS–fertility hypothesis; abstract missing but title and llm_reason are unambiguous.
scoreRationale: Natural experiment exploiting Bismarck's 1881 social insurance introduction to test causal effect on Prussian marriage and fertility 1881–1910 — directly targets the OAS–fertility hypothesis; abstract missing but title and llm_reason are unambiguous.

title: The fertility effects of public pension: Evidence from the new rural pension scheme in China
mechanism: DID with propensity score matching, fixed effects, and IV approach estimating the causal effect of China's New Rural Pension Scheme expansion on married women's fertility in rural China — directly tests the core hypothesis with strong identification and OAS-fertility as the primary research question.
scoreRationale: DID with propensity score matching, fixed effects, and IV approach estimating the causal effect of China's New Rural Pension Scheme expansion on married women's fertility in rural China — directly tests the core hypothesis with strong identification and OAS-fertility as the primary research question.

title: The intergenerational education spillovers of pension reform
mechanism: Uses Chilean pension reform as a natural experiment to causally identify intergenerational spillovers on children's education enrollment — credible quasi-experimental identification of OAS-reform effects on family investment behavior, with OAS-fertility as a main tested hypothesis.
scoreRationale: Uses Chilean pension reform as a natural experiment to causally identify intergenerational spillovers on children's education enrollment — credible quasi-experimental identification of OAS-reform effects on family investment behavior, with OAS-fertility as a main tested hypothesis.

title: Retirement, intergenerational time transfers, and fertility
mechanism: Uses RDD on retirement eligibility ages and DID on German pension reforms to estimate causal effect of parental retirement on adult children's fertility; quasi-experimental identification is credible, though the primary outcome is intergenerational time transfers rather than pension-as-OAS crowdout per se.
scoreRationale: Uses RDD on retirement eligibility ages and DID on German pension reforms to estimate causal effect of parental retirement on adult children's fertility; quasi-experimental identification is credible, though the primary outcome is intergenerational time transfers rather than pension-as-OAS crowdout per se.

title: Children as insurance revisited: Impact of children on private insurance adoption among older parents
mechanism: Uses the 'Later, Longer, Fewer' campaign as an IV for number of children to estimate the causal effect on private insurance adoption — directly tests the children-as-insurance substitution hypothesis with credible quasi-experimental identification.
scoreRationale: Uses the 'Later, Longer, Fewer' campaign as an IV for number of children to estimate the causal effect on private insurance adoption — directly tests the children-as-insurance substitution hypothesis with credible quasi-experimental identification.

title: Fertility and parental retirement
mechanism: Uses a sharp RDD on the 2006 Dutch pension reform (birth-date cutoff) to show that delayed parental retirement reduces daughters' fertility through reduced grandparental childcare supply — credible quasi-experimental identification with pension reform as the exogenous shock; OAS–fertility is a primary finding though the mechanism is grandchild-care rather than classic crowdout.
scoreRationale: Uses a sharp RDD on the 2006 Dutch pension reform (birth-date cutoff) to show that delayed parental retirement reduces daughters' fertility through reduced grandparental childcare supply — credible quasi-experimental identification with pension reform as the exogenous shock; OAS–fertility is a primary finding though the mechanism is grandchild-care rather than classic crowdout.

title: Are children substitutes for assets? Evidence from Bangladesh
mechanism: Uses a family planning programme as an IV for fertility to test the children-as-assets substitution hypothesis in Bangladesh — credible quasi-experimental identification with OAS-fertility substitution as a central hypothesis.
scoreRationale: Uses a family planning programme as an IV for fertility to test the children-as-assets substitution hypothesis in Bangladesh — credible quasi-experimental identification with OAS-fertility substitution as a central hypothesis.

title: Children's education and parental old age survival - Quasi-experimental evidence on the intergenerational effects of human capital investment
mechanism: Quasi-experimental study using the 1974 Tanzania education reform as an IV to estimate effects of children's schooling on parental survival — strong identification but OAS-fertility crowdout is a secondary implication, not the primary question (focus is child education → parental health).
scoreRationale: Quasi-experimental study using the 1974 Tanzania education reform as an IV to estimate effects of children's schooling on parental survival — strong identification but OAS-fertility crowdout is a secondary implication, not the primary question (focus is child education → parental health).

title: Grandparental availability for child care and maternal labor force participation: pension reform evidence from Italy
mechanism: Exploits pension reform-induced variation in grandmothers' retirement eligibility in Italy to identify the effect on daughters' labor force participation (not directly fertility); strong quasi-experimental design but fertility is not the outcome, making OAS–fertility centrality secondary.
scoreRationale: Exploits pension reform-induced variation in grandmothers' retirement eligibility in Italy to identify the effect on daughters' labor force participation (not directly fertility); strong quasi-experimental design but fertility is not the outcome, making OAS–fertility centrality secondary.

title: Grandmothers and Granddaughters: Old Age Pension and Intra-household Allocation in South Africa
mechanism: Exploits the 1990s expansion of South Africa's social pension as a natural experiment to estimate effects on child anthropometric outcomes; strong quasi-experimental identification but fertility is not an outcome — pension effects on child welfare are studied, not OAS crowdout of fertility.
scoreRationale: Exploits the 1990s expansion of South Africa's social pension as a natural experiment to estimate effects on child anthropometric outcomes; strong quasi-experimental identification but fertility is not an outcome — pension effects on child welfare are studied, not OAS crowdout of fertility.

title: Pension Policy Reform and Fertility: Micro Evidence from Ghana
mechanism: Abstract missing; exploits a natural experiment (PAYG pension reform in Ghana) to causally identify the effect of old-age security on fertility, with the OAS–fertility link as the explicit primary research question — top scores on all three dimensions.
scoreRationale: Abstract missing; exploits a natural experiment (PAYG pension reform in Ghana) to causally identify the effect of old-age security on fertility, with the OAS–fertility link as the explicit primary research question — top scores on all three dimensions.

title: Pensions and Fertility: Evidence from Germany
mechanism: Abstract missing; title 'Pensions and Fertility: Evidence from Germany' signals a quasi-experimental or IV study exploiting German pension policy variation, with the OAS-fertility causal effect as the primary research question — maximum score on all dimensions.
scoreRationale: Abstract missing; title 'Pensions and Fertility: Evidence from Germany' signals a quasi-experimental or IV study exploiting German pension policy variation, with the OAS-fertility causal effect as the primary research question — maximum score on all dimensions.

title: Pension Reforms and Fertility: Evidence from a Natural Experiment in Italy
mechanism: Natural experiment using Italian pension reform to identify the causal effect on fertility — highest evidence type and identification quality; OAS-fertility is the sole research question; abstract appears to be a metadata error but title and llm_reason are unambiguous.
scoreRationale: Natural experiment using Italian pension reform to identify the causal effect on fertility — highest evidence type and identification quality; OAS-fertility is the sole research question; abstract appears to be a metadata error but title and llm_reason are unambiguous.

title: Fertility and Financial Development: Evidence from U.S. Counties in the 19th Century
scoreRationale: Tests OAS hypothesis on 19th-c US counties; IV (1820 bank presence) plus PA legal exogeneity give credible causal negative effect of finance on fertility. Primary focus.

title: The intergenerational impact of pension reforms: How grandmothers’ pension eligibility affects daughters’ fertility
scoreRationale: Australian pension-eligibility reform (age +7yr) as natural experiment; causal decline in daughters' fertility via reduced grandmother childcare; core.

title: Relationship between Receipt of a Social Protection Grant for a Child and Second Pregnancy Rates among South African Women: A Cohort Study
scoreRationale: Cohort study plus natural experiment (2003 age-eligibility expansion) on grant receipt and second pregnancy; grant->fertility is core.

title: Children, old-age support and pension in rural China
scoreRationale: China ORPP DID on sex ratio; estimates pension as substitute for children in old-age support. Clean quasi-experiment.

title: The Long-Term Consequences of Having Fewer Children in Old Age: Evidence from China’s “Later, Longer, Fewer” Campaign
scoreRationale: Exploits provincial variation in China's Later-Longer-Fewer campaign; causal effect of fewer children on old-age support (reverse OAS chain).

title: Pension Coverage for Parents and Educational Investment in Children: Evidence from Urban China
mechanism: pension expansion DiD child investment China
scoreRationale: Quasi-experiment (urban China pension expansion), DiD+IV on child education spending; OAS mechanism, outcome not fertility.

title: The US baby boom and the 1935 Social Security Act
scoreRationale: Exploits cross-state variation in 1935 SSA social spending on fertility via census + state parity panel; policy variation, but exogeneity not fully clean.

title: The One-Child Policy and Household Saving
scoreRationale: Life-cycle model + twins-under-one-child-policy quasi-experiment; fewer kids lower old-age support, raise saving. Strong ID, mechanism central.

title: The Impact of Social Pensions on Intergenerational Relationships: Comparative Evidence from China
scoreRationale: China NRPS fuzzy RD; pension reduces coresidence and weakens intergenerational transfers/old-age support. Strong ID, fertility-adjacent.

title: Is There an Effect of Incremental Welfare Benefits on Fertility Behavior? A Look at the Family Cap
scoreRationale: Exploits cross-state timing of family-cap policy on vital-stats births; quasi-experimental, finds no fertility decline; benefits not pensions.

title: Rearranging the Family?
scoreRationale: South Africa pension age discontinuity (RD); shifts coresidence toward childbearing-age women and young children. Fertility-adjacent.

title: Do Public Transfers Crowd Out Private Support to the Elderly? Evidence from a Randomized Experiment in Ecuador
mechanism: Abstract confirms a randomized experiment (Basic Old Age Pension in Korea) using DID to estimate crowdout of private family transfers by public pensions — strong quasi-experimental identification, but outcome is private transfers to the elderly rather than fertility directly.
scoreRationale: Abstract confirms a randomized experiment (Basic Old Age Pension in Korea) using DID to estimate crowdout of private family transfers by public pensions — strong quasi-experimental identification, but outcome is private transfers to the elderly rather than fertility directly.

title: Does the Expansion of Kindergarten Supply Increase Fertility？Quasi-natural Experimental Evidence from Establishing Kindergartens by Social Forces
scoreRationale: Quasi-natural experiment (China kindergarten policy, DID+IV) raising fertility via lower childcare cost; strong design but OAS/pension tangential.

title: OLD‐AGE PENSION AND EXTENDED FAMILIES: HOW IS ADULT CHILDREN'S INTERNAL MIGRATION AFFECTED?
scoreRationale: Fuzzy RD on rural China social pension reform; strong ID but outcome is adult-children migration, not fertility.

title: Do Cash Transfers Improve Birth Outcomes? Evidence from Matched Vital Statistics, Program and Social Security Data
scoreRationale: Matched admin+vital-stats micro-data on cash transfer, causal; fertility explicitly checked (not driver) but outcome is birth weight.

title: The impact of non-contributory pensions : a case study for Costa Rica
scoreRationale: Non-contributory pension Costa Rica; DD + fuzzy RD/diff-in-disc on age discontinuity. Strong design but outcomes are labor/household, fertility peripheral.

title: The Effect of Financial Resources on Homeownership, Marriage, and Fertility: Evidence from State Lotteries
scoreRationale: State-lottery natural experiment on fertility/marriage/homeownership; modest fertility effect. Strong ID but income shock, not OAS.

title: Does Social Security Crowd Out Private Savings?
scoreRationale: Prussian DID showing Bismarck social insurance crowded out private savings; strong ID but outcome is savings, not fertility.

title: The Impact of Rural Pensions in China on Labor Migration
scoreRationale: RD on China rural pension eligibility; strong design but outcome is adult-child labor migration/elderly care, not fertility.

title: Traditional Institutions in Modern Times: Dowries as Pensions When Sons Migrate
scoreRationale: Quasi-experiment (highway program) on dowry as old-age support enabling son migration. Strong ID but outcome is migration, not fertility; OAS mechanism one theme.

title: The Effect of Public Pension Wealth on Saving and Expenditure
scoreRationale: Poland 1999 reform quasi-experiment, cohort-by-time variation on saving. Clean ID but outcome is saving not fertility.

title: The impact of parental leave policy on the intensity of labour-market participation of mothers: Do the number of children and pre-birth work engagement matter?
scoreRationale: Clean DiD on 1999 parental-leave reform, but outcome is maternal labor hours, not OAS-fertility crowdout.

title: Will You Marry Me, Later?
scoreRationale: Staggered DiD on Mexican min-marriage-age reform, effects on marriage/fertility; clean design but unrelated to OAS.

title: Labor Informality and the Incentive Effects of Social Security: Evidence from a Health Reform in Uruguay
scoreRationale: DiD on Uruguay health-benefit reform; strong ID but outcome is labor informality, not fertility.

title: Female Employment Reduces Fertility in Rural Senegal
scoreRationale: IV+DID+PSM on female employment reducing fertility in Senegal; strong ID but about employment, no OAS/pension link.

title: The child penalty: evidence from Spain
scoreRationale: Event-study on Spanish social-security records estimating earnings child penalty; strong design but no OAS->fertility content, tangential.

title: Health and labor market effects of an unanticipated rise in retirement age. Evidence from the 2012 Italian pension reform
scoreRationale: DiD on 2012 Italian pension reform raising retirement age, health/labor outcomes on admin data; strong design but no fertility outcome.
